title: Init & Commits: Giving Your Code a History
learningObjectives: Initialize a Git repository and explain what .git/ actually stores
Stage specific files/changes with git add before committing
Write a commit message that explains why a change was made, not just what changed
Use git log and git diff to inspect history instead of guessing what changed
whyItMatters: The digital_twin/ package you just built across the Python lessons has no history — if you break it tomorrow, you have no way back to today's working version. Every AI Energy OS module from here on is real, evolving software; software without version history is one accidental edit away from being unrecoverable.
capstoneContribution: The first commit of the Digital Twin's actual version history — the point where 'a folder of Python files' becomes 'a project with a recoverable past.'

problem.summary: Working code with no recorded history has no way back if you break it.
problem.content: Your `digital_twin/` package works right now. If you make a risky change tomorrow and break it, you have no way to get back to today's exact working version.

motivation.summary: Unrecorded work is one accidental edit away from being lost.
motivation.content: This is the single most common reason engineers lose work: an edit that seemed safe, followed by no way back. Git's entire value proposition is that every meaningful state of your code is recoverable, as long as you actually commit it.

theory.summary: init creates history tracking; add stages deliberate changes; commit records a permanent snapshot.
theory.content: `git init` creates a `.git/` folder that tracks history for the current directory. `git add <file>` stages specific changes for the next commit — staging lets you commit a deliberate, reviewed set of changes rather than 'everything that happens to be different right now.' `git commit -m "message"` records a permanent snapshot of the staged changes. A good commit message explains *why*, not just *what*: 'fix classify_demand off-by-one at exactly 5000kW' beats 'fix bug.'

visualExplanation.summary: Working directory, staging area, and history are three distinct zones files move through.
visualExplanation.content: The working directory, the staging area, and the commit history are shown as three distinct zones, with files moving left-to-right as `git add` then `git commit` are run — clarifying that editing a file doesn't automatically stage or commit it.

interactiveDemo.summary: Edit, stage, and commit a file and watch it move through all three zones.
interactiveDemo.content: Edit a file in a simulated working directory, watch it appear as 'modified, not staged,' run a staging command and watch it move to 'staged,' then commit and watch it appear in a growing commit history timeline.

guidedCoding.summary: Follow along: initialize a repo and make your first real commit.
guidedCoding.content:
```bash
git init
git add digital_twin/
git commit -m "Add initial digital_twin package: ingestion, classification, reporting"

git log --oneline
git diff HEAD~1 HEAD
```

independentCoding.summary: Make and commit a real, explained change to your own package.
independentCoding.content: Make a small, real change to one file in your `digital_twin/` package (e.g. adjust a threshold constant), stage only that file, and commit it with a message that explains why the threshold changed.

debugging.summary: Diagnose a commit that reports 'nothing to commit' because staging was skipped.
debugging.content: A learner ran `git commit -m "update thresholds"` and got `nothing to commit, working tree clean` even though they'd clearly edited a file. Identify the missing step and fix the workflow.

reflection.summary: Staging is a deliberate checkpoint, not busywork.
reflection.content: Why does Git require an explicit staging step instead of just committing whatever is different in the working directory?

teachBack.summary: Explain modified vs. staged vs. committed using your own repository.
teachBack.content: Explain to someone the difference between 'modified,' 'staged,' and 'committed' using your own `digital_twin/` package as the example.

miniProject.summary: Turn the accumulated 01-python project into a real Git repository.
miniProject.content: Turn your entire accumulated 01-python mini project into a real Git repository: `git init`, an initial commit of everything as it stands today, and one follow-up commit making and recording a real improvement.

portfolioEvidence.summary: This is the first commit in the Digital Twin's permanent history.
portfolioEvidence.content: This is the first commit in the Digital Twin's permanent, recoverable history — everything from here on has a paper trail.

labs.title: First History
labs.description: Sandboxed exercise requiring the learner to initialize a repository, stage specific files deliberately, and produce a commit history with meaningful, why-focused messages.

simulations.title: Staging Area Visualizer
simulations.description: Shows files moving between working directory, staging area, and commit history as add/commit commands run.

flashcards.front: What does git add actually do, if it doesn't create a commit?
flashcards.back: It stages a specific file's current changes for the next commit, letting you build a deliberate, reviewed set of changes instead of committing everything different in the working directory at once.

flashcards.front: What makes 'fix classify_demand off-by-one at exactly 5000kW' a better commit message than 'fix bug'?
flashcards.back: It explains what specifically was wrong and where, so anyone reading the history later (including future you) understands why the change happened without having to re-derive it from the diff alone.

rubric: Commit messages explain why a change was made, not just restate what files changed
Only deliberately intended changes are staged and committed, not incidental unrelated edits
Learner can correctly use git log and git diff to answer 'what changed and why' about their own history

references.title: Pro Git — Getting Started: Git Basics
